comments.author: @RobinLee-u4l
comments.text: Laos is one of the most astounding countries that I have ever visited.  Though heavily bombed by the USA, I was still able to enjoy some of its unique culture before it was invaded by the Chinese. Much is being destroyed, but I hope the Laotians can preserve what they can of their own heritage.

comments.author: @FalmerBill
comments.text:
An extraordinary story with excellent narration. The story of the Hmong people and the unexploded ordinances brought tears to this old man's eyes. I can't help but imagine it being compared to a neutral Switzerland being bombarded to extreme levels by both Allies and Axis powers. 

Laos, a beautiful country with nature beauty and resources slowly losing it's identity to the world's superpower, one at a time. Sobering reminder of human nature through time immemorial. Through it all, there remains hope for a brighter future. May the new generations make wiser decisions than many of our forefathers ever did.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Thank you for sitting with that part of the story the way you did — the Hmong chapter is one of the heaviest we've told, and comparisons like yours help show just how disproportionate it really was. Glad it reached you.

comments.author: @laurelsternberg5861
comments.text: Oh my gosh! Did not know this history. A gorgeous land, but what a hideous betrayal by the CIA! And so much unexploded ordinance left! I live in central Israel. Our local beach was recently closed for a day while the army cleared "debris", which was  yet unexploded. We know the sound of cluster bombs. I've learned things about explosives I never wanted to know.

comments.author: @jamesl9371
comments.text:
I’ve been to Siem Reap and they have the same problem with the bombs and land mines.
Some people have trained rats to search for and find the land mines

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: The trained rats (HeroRATs, if that's what you're thinking of) are an incredible program - light enough not to trigger the mines, and remarkably effective. Thank you for adding the cross-border context.

comments.author: @jamesl9371
comments.text:
In the 1980’s I was working as a chef in a French restaurant in Toronto 
We had a Vietnamese couple stay in our house. “Boat people “
A woman from Laos worked in our restaurant bussing tables. She was also a refugee 
She had a young daughter who I took ice skating ⛸️ at Toronto City Hall 
Now so many years later I’m living in Vietnam and hope to visit Laos

replies.author: @jamesl9371
replies.text:
@WhereonEarth4KI have not been to Laos yet. Unfortunately the flights are more expensive and it’s not really easy to get there from here. Hopefully I’ll go someday. I’ve been to Bangkok many times (cheap flights) and Kuala Lumpur and Taipei and Hong Kong and mainland China and Cambodia. 
By the way a great thing about Canada is the multi cultural society. Toronto is the most multicultural city I’ve been in. Half of the people were not even born in Canada. It’s a good thing. Lots of diversity and good restaurants. Meanwhile I like being in Asia. Also good people and good food

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: That's a remarkable thread to have followed — from bussing tables and ice skating in Toronto to living in Vietnam decades later. Have you made it to Laos yet, or is that still on the list?

comments.author: @TheBuckwoody
comments.text: I was in the jungle driving around in a Soviet Zil 131 in 2002 watching 7 divisions of Vietnamese soldiers surrounding Hmong freedom fighters, with Vietnamese Generals staring at me eating Pho'  in small villages wondering why some American looking guy was driving around in a Soviet Truck.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: That scene — a Soviet truck, Vietnamese generals, Hmong fighters, and Pho in a jungle village in 2002 — is the kind of detail no documentary ever captures. Thank you for sharing it. 🌿

comments.author: @TheBuckwoody
comments.text:
1. There was a political agreement — the 1973 Vientiane Agreement
This agreement was supposed to:
end fighting between Royal Lao Government and Pathet Lao
freeze troop positions
require all foreign troops to leave Laos
create a coalition government
Both sides signed it.
But here’s the key:
❗ North Vietnam never withdrew its troops
They publicly agreed to withdraw.
They privately refused.
They continued reinforcing Pathet Lao positions.

comments.author: @TheBuckwoody
comments.text:
Before 1973:
NVA already had 50,000–70,000 troops inside Laos (combat + logistics).
After U.S. withdrawal (1973–1975):
Vietnam moved:
entire NVA divisions
engineering regiments
anti‑aircraft brigades
political cadres
logistics battalions
security forces
Pathet Lao reinforcements trained in Vietnam

into:
Saravane
Sekong
Attopeu
Xam Neua
Phong Saly
Bolovens Plateau
the entire eastern half of Laos

❗ The total Vietnamese communist presence in Laos by 1975 was well over 100,000.
Some intelligence estimates — especially Thai and French — put the number at:

⭐ 150,000–200,000 Vietnamese troops and cadres
inside Laos by the time the Pathet Lao took full control in December 1975.

This includes:
combat troops
logistics troops
political officers
security forces
trail construction units
anti‑aircraft units
NVA units stationed permanently along the Trail

So yes — hundreds of thousands is not unreasonable when counting all categories of personnel.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Actually, Laos is firmly part of Southeast Asia — it shares borders with Vietnam, Cambodia, Thailand, Myanmar, and China, all within the region. Might be thinking of a different classification.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Good additions — Mongolia and Moldova both belong on that landlocked list too. Thanks for rounding it out.

comments.author: @no42arak-st-floor44

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Absolutely — the landlocked geography of Central and South Asia tells its own remarkable story. Laos just happens to wear it in a particularly vivid way.
